slides.title: What SOQL is
body: SOQL = Salesforce Object Query Language. It's how Apex (and Flow, and Reports) reads data out of the Salesforce database.
Real example: looks like SQL but specialized for Salesforce — every CRM and ORM in the world has a similar query language. If you know basic SQL, SOQL feels familiar.

vocabulary.def: Salesforce Object Query Language — Apex's database query syntax.

slides.title: The basic SELECT shape
body: SELECT field1, field2 FROM ObjectName. List the fields you want, then the object to pull from.
Apex wraps SOQL in square brackets: [SELECT Id, Name FROM Account]. The brackets are how Apex knows it's SOQL, not regular code.

slides.title: Your first SOQL query
slides.intro: Type this. The [...] brackets are inline SOQL — runs against your org and returns a List.
lineExplanations: Inline SOQL inside [...]. Returns up to 5 Account records as a List<Account>. LIMIT keeps you safe from accidentally pulling millions of rows.
Read the Name field. We asked for Id and Name in the SELECT, so those are the only fields populated on each record.

slides.title: Filtering with WHERE
body: WHERE narrows the results. Only rows matching the condition come back.
Real example: 'all Accounts with annual revenue over $1M' is a WHERE clause. Every Salesforce report is built on top of one of these.

vocabulary.def: SOQL clause that filters results to rows matching a condition.

slides.title: WHERE clause
slides.intro: Type this. Only Accounts in California come back.
lineExplanations: Variable receives the query result. The opening [ starts the SOQL.
WHERE filters: only rows where BillingState equals 'CA'. Single quotes for the literal — same rule as String literals.

slides.title: ORDER BY and operators
body: ORDER BY sorts. Operators in WHERE: = != > < >= <= LIKE IN.
Real example: a sales rep dashboard might query 'opportunities in my pipeline ordered by close date desc' — that's ORDER BY CloseDate DESC.

slides.title: Sorted, filtered query
slides.intro: Type this. Returns the top 10 newest closed-won opportunities.
slides.code:
List<Opportunity> wins = [
    SELECT Id, Name, Amount, CloseDate
    FROM Opportunity
    WHERE StageName = 'Closed Won' AND Amount > 10000
    ORDER BY CloseDate DESC
    LIMIT 10
];
lineExplanations: Two conditions joined by AND. Both must be true.

slides.title: Bind variables — using Apex values in SOQL
body: To use a variable inside a SOQL query, prefix it with a colon: WHERE Id = :recordId. This is called a 'bind' — Apex substitutes the value safely.
Real example: every dynamic query in production code uses binds. NEVER concatenate strings into SOQL — that's the SOQL-injection equivalent and Apex security review will block your package.

vocabulary.def: An Apex variable inserted into a SOQL query with the : prefix. Safe and substituted by the platform.

slides.title: Querying with a bind
slides.intro: Type this. The :acctId in the WHERE clause is a bind variable.
lineExplanations: Declare an Id holding a specific Account record's id.
WHERE AccountId = :acctId — bind the Apex variable into the query.
Print how many contacts came back.

slides.title: Parent-to-child relationships (subquery)
body: From an Account, get all its Contacts in one query: SELECT Name, (SELECT Email FROM Contacts) FROM Account.
Real example: a customer-360 dashboard wants 'this account and all its contacts' in one round-trip. One query is faster than one-per-account.

vocabulary.def: A nested SOQL inside a parent query that pulls related child records.

slides.title: Account with its Contacts
slides.intro: Type this. The (subquery) pulls all Contacts under each Account.
lineExplanations: SELECT main fields PLUS (subquery for Contacts). Note 'Contacts' is plural — that's the relationship name.
a.Contacts is now a List<Contact>. Use .size() to count without another query.

slides.title: Child-to-parent (dot notation)
body: From a Contact, walk UP to its Account: SELECT Account.Name FROM Contact.
Real example: a contact-list view that shows the parent company name beside each contact. One query, no second lookup.

slides.title: Contact with parent Account
slides.intro: Type this. Walking from Contact to its parent Account uses the dot.
lineExplanations: Account.Name and Account.BillingCity walk up the relationship to the parent's fields.
WHERE clause can also walk up — filter contacts whose parent Account is in Tech.

slides.front: What's wrong with this code? String name = 'Acme'; List<Account> a = [SELECT Id FROM Account WHERE Name = ' + name + ' ];
slides.back: It uses string concatenation instead of a bind variable.
slides.detail: Use :name instead. Concatenated SOQL is a security hole and Salesforce review will reject it.

slides.question: You want every Account's name AND a list of every contact's email under each account, in ONE query. Which clause?
slides.explanation: Subquery in the SELECT: SELECT Name, (SELECT Email FROM Contacts) FROM Account. One round-trip, no governor-limit pressure from looping queries.

slides.title: Capstone: Account 360 query
slides.intro: Sales reps want a one-shot account-360 view — the Account's industry context PLUS its child contacts ordered by last name, all in a single round-trip. Build the query and unpack the results without making a second SOQL call.
lineExplanations: An Account Id — could be passed in, looked up, or hardcoded for testing.
Multi-line SOQL inside square brackets. Use a List<Account> even when you expect 0 or 1 rows.
Industry — one of the most-used picklists for segmentation.
Subquery for child Contacts. 'Contacts' (plural) is the relationship name on Account. ORDER BY sorts the children.
Bind the Apex variable into the query with :accountId. NEVER concatenate.
Cap at 1 row — we expect a single Account.
Guard before reading from the list — empty results would NPE on results[0].
Walk the child Contacts — already loaded by the subquery, no additional SOQL.

slides.title: What you now know
body: SELECT, WHERE, ORDER BY, LIMIT, bind variables, parent-to-child subqueries, and child-to-parent dot notation.
Next: DML — how Apex WRITES data back to the Salesforce database (insert, update, delete).
